briefdescription.para: The addition function between two UniTensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the addition function for UniTensor. It will perform the element-wise addition. That means if the left UniTensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the right UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_L[i] + T_R[i], \] where $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_L $ and $ T_R $. It will perform the element-wise addition and note that it will return a new UniTensor object.
Lt
The left UniTensor.
Rt
The right UniTensor.
The result UniTensor.
Lt and Rt must have the same shape.
UniTensor::Add(const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt) const, operator+(const cytnx::UniTensor &Lt, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The addition function between a template type and a UniTensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the addition function for UniTensor. It will add the UniTensor and a template type together and add every element in the UniTensor with the template type. That means if the template type lc is given as $ c $ and the UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_i $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = c + T_i[i], \] where $ T_i[i] $ and $ T_o[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_i $ and $ T_o $.
lc
The left template type.
supported type: Scalar, cytnx::cytnx_complex128, cytnx::cytnx_complex64, cytnx::cytnx_double, cytnx::cytnx_float, cytnx::cytnx_int64, cytnx::cytnx_uint64, cytnx::cytnx_int32, cytnx::cytnx_uint32, cytnx::cytnx_int16, cytnx::cytnx_uint16, cytnx::cytnx_bool.
Rt
The right UniTensor.
The result UniTensor.
The supported template type shown above.
The inpute template type lc will be casted to the same type as the UniTensor Rt.
operator+(const T &lc, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt), Add(const cytnx::UniTensor &Lt, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The addition function between a UniTensor and a template type.

detaileddescription.para: This is the addition function for UniTensor. It will add the UniTensor and a template type together and add every element in the UniTensor with the template type. That means if the UniTensor Lt is given as $ T_i $ and the template type rc is given as $ c $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_i[i] + c, \] where $ T_i[i] $ and $ T_o[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_i $ and $ T_o $.

parameterdescription.para: The left UniTensor.

parblock.para: supported type: Scalar, cytnx::cytnx_complex128, cytnx::cytnx_complex64, cytnx::cytnx_double, cytnx::cytnx_float, cytnx::cytnx_int64, cytnx::cytnx_uint64, cytnx::cytnx_int32, cytnx::cytnx_uint32, cytnx::cytnx_int16, cytnx::cytnx_uint16, cytnx::cytnx_bool.

simplesect.para: The result UniTensor.
The supported template type shown above.
The inpute template type rc will be casted to the same type as the UniTensor Lt.
operator+(const cytnx::UniTensor &Lt, const T &rc), Add(const T &lc, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The subtraction function between two UniTensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the subtraction function for UniTensor. It will subtract the UniTensor and a template type together. It will subtract every element in the UniTensor with the template type. That means if the UniTensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_L[i] - T_R[i], \] where $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_L $ and $ T_R $. It will perform the element-wise subtraction and note that it will return a new UniTensor object.
Lt
The left UniTensor.
Rt
The right UniTensor.
The result UniTensor.
Lt and Rt must have the same shape.
UniTensor::Sub(const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt) const, operator-(const cytnx::UniTensor &Lt, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The subtraction function between a UniTensor and a template type.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the subtraction function for UniTensor. It will subtract the UniTensor and a template type together. It will subtract every element in the UniTensor with the template type. That means if the template type lc is given as $ c $ and the UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_i $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = c - T_i[i], \] where $ T_i[i] $ and $ T_o[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_i $ and $ T_o $.

The left UniTensor.
rc
The right template type.

operator-(const T &lc, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt), Sub(const T &lc, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The multiplication function between two UniTensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the multiplication function for UniTensor. It will multiply the two UniTensor together. It will multiply every element in the UniTensor Lt with the corresponding element in the UniTensor Rt. That means if the UniTensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_L[i] \times T_R[i], \] where $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_L $ and $ T_R $. It will perform the element-wise multiplication and note that it will return a new UniTensor object.
Lt
The left UniTensor.
Rt
The right UniTensor.
The result UniTensor.
Lt and Rt must have the same shape.
UniTensor::Mul(const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt) const, operator*(const cytnx::UniTensor &Lt, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

That means if the template type lc is given as $ c $ and the UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_i $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = c \times T_i[i], \] where $ T_i[i] $ and $ T_o[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_i $ and $ T_o $.
Lt
The left UniTensor.
rc
The right template type.

operator*(const T &lc, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt), Mul(const T &lc, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The division function between two UniTensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the division function for UniTensor. It will divide the left UniTensor and the right UniTensor together. It will divide every element in the left UniTensor with the right UniTensor. That means if the left UniTensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the right UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_L[i] \div T_R[i], \] where $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_L $ and $ T_R $. It will perform the element-wise division and note that it will return a new UniTensor object.
Lt
The left UniTensor.
Rt
The right UniTensor.
The result UniTensor.
Lt and Rt must have the same shape.
UniTensor::Div(const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt) const, operator/(const cytnx::UniTensor &Lt, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: element-wise modulo

detaileddescription.para: The modulo function between two UniTensor.

This is the modulo function for UniTensor. It will perform the element-wise modulo between two UniTensor. That means if the UniTensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the UniTensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_L[i] \mod T_R[i], \] where $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the UniTensor $ T_L $ and $ T_R $.
Lt
The left UniTensor.
Rt
The right UniTensor.
The result UniTensor.
Lt and Rt must have the same shape.
The input UniTensor Lt and Rt need to be integer type.
Tensor::Mod(const T &rhs), operator%(const cytnx::UniTensor &Lt, const cytnx::UniTensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: Perform Singular-Value decomposition on a UniTensor using divide-and-conquer method.

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs the Singular-Value decomposition on a UniTensor Tin. The result will depend on the rowrank of the UniTensor Tin. For more details, please refer to the documentation of the function Svd(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_UvT).
Svd(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_UvT)

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs the Singular-Value decomposition on a UniTensor Tin. The result will depend on the rowrank of the UniTensor Tin. For more details, please refer to the documentation of the functions Gesvd(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_U, const bool &is_vT) and Svd(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_UvT).
Gesvd(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_U, const bool &is_vT), Svd(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_UvT).

memberdef.name: Svd_truncate

briefdescription.para: Perform Singular-Value decomposition on a UniTensor with truncation.

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs the Singular-Value decomposition of a UniTensor Tin and truncates the singular values. The result will depend on the rowrank of the UniTensor Tin. For more details, please refer to the references below.
Svd_truncate(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const std::vector<cytnx_uint64> min_blockdim, const double &err, const bool &is_UvT, const unsigned int &return_err, const cytnx_uint64 &mindim), Svd_truncate(const Tensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const double &err, const bool &is_UvT, const unsigned int &return_err, const cytnx_uint64& mindim)

memberdef.argsstring: (const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, std::vector< cytnx_uint64 > min_blockdim, const double &err=0., const bool &is_UvT=true, const unsigned int &return_err=0, const cytnx_uint64 &mindim=1)
memberdef.name: Svd_truncate

briefdescription.para: Perform Singular-Value decomposition on a UniTensor with truncation and keep at most min_blockdim singular values in each block.

detaileddescription.para: This function performs the Singular-Value decomposition of a UniTensor Tin and truncates the singular values. The result will depend on the rowrank of the UniTensor Tin. For each block, the minimum dimension can be chosen. This can be helpful to avoid loosing symmetry sectors in the truncated SVD. For more details, please refer to the documentation of the function Svd_truncate(const Tensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const double &err, const bool &is_UvT, const unsigned int &return_err).

The truncation order is as following (later constraints might be violated by previous ones): 1) Keep the largest min_blockdim singular values in each block; reduce keepdim and mindim by the number of already kept singular values 2) Keep at most keepdim singular values; there might be an exception in case of exact degeneracies where more singular values are kept 3) Keep at least mindim singular values; 4) Drop all singular values smaller than err (no normalization applied to the singular values)

parameterdescription.para: a BlockUniTensor, with the correct rowrank set to interpret it as a matrix
the number (at most) of singular values to keep.
a vector containing the minimum dimension of each block; alternatively, a vector with only one element can be given to have the same min_blockdim for each block
the cutoff error (the singular values smaller than err will be truncated.)
if true, the left- and right- unitary UniTensors (isometries) are returned.

whether the error shall be returned. If return_err is true, then the largest error will be pushed back to the vector (The smallest singular value in the return singular values matrix $ S $.) If return_err is a positive int, then the full list of truncated singular values will be returned.

listitem.para: The first UniTensor is a diagonal UniTensor containing the singular values
If is_UvT is true, then the UniTensor $ U $ and $ V^\dagger $ will be pushed back to the vector.
If return_err is true, then the error will be pushed back to the vector.

simplesect.para: This function assumes a BlockUniTensor as input for Tin.

para.ref: Svd_truncate(const Tensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const double &err, const bool &is_UvT, const unsigned int &return_err, const cytnx_uint64& mindim)

simplesect.para: The truncated bond dimension can be larger than keepdim for degenerate singular values: if the largest $ n $ truncated singular values would be exactly equal to the smallest kept singular value, then the bond dimension is enlarged to keepdim $ + n $. Example: if the singular values are (1 2 2 2 2 3) and keepdim = 3, then the bond dimension will be 5 in order to keep all the degenerate singular values.

memberdef.argsstring: (const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const double &err=0., const bool &is_U=true, const bool &is_vT=true, const unsigned int &return_err=0, const cytnx_uint64 &mindim=1)
memberdef.name: Gesvd_truncate

briefdescription.para: Perform Singular-Value decomposition on a UniTensor with truncation.

This function performs the Singular-Value decomposition of a UniTensor Tin and truncates the singular values. The result will depend on the rowrank of the UniTensor Tin. This version uses the ?gesvd method. See references below for more details.
Svd_truncate(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const std::vector<cytnx_uint64> min_blockdim, const double &err, const bool &is_UvT, const unsigned int &return_err, const cytnx_uint64 &mindim), Gesvd(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const bool &is_U, const bool &is_vT)

memberdef.name: Gesvd_truncate

briefdescription.para: Perform Singular-Value decomposition on a UniTensor with truncation and keep at most min_blockdim singular values in each block.

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs the Singular-Value decomposition of a UniTensor Tin and truncates the singular values. This version uses the ?gesvd method. See references below for more details.
Svd_truncate(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const std::vector<cytnx_uint64> min_blockdim, const double &err, const bool &is_UvT, const unsigned int &return_err, const cytnx_uint64 &mindim), Gesvd(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const bool &is_U, const bool &is_vT)

memberdef.argsstring: (const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, cytnx_uint64 keepdim, double err=0., bool is_U=true, bool is_vT=true, unsigned int return_err=0, cytnx_uint64 mindim=1, cytnx_uint64 oversampling_summand=10, double oversampling_factor=1., cytnx_uint64 power_iteration=0, unsigned int seed=random::__static_random_device())
memberdef.name: Rsvd

briefdescription.para: Perform a randomized truncated Singular-Value decomposition of a UniTensor.

detaileddescription.para: This function will perform a randomized truncated Singular-Value decomposition of a UniTensor. It uses the ?gesvd method for the SVD. It will perform the randomized SVD first, and then truncate the singular values to the given cutoff err. That means, given a UniTensor Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = U S V^\dagger, \] where $ S $ is a singular values matrix with the singular values truncated to the given cutoff err. The dimension of $ S $ is at most keepdim.

The truncation order is as following (later constraints might be violated by previous ones): 1) Keep at most keepdim singular values; there might be an exception in case of exact degeneracies, see note below 2) Keep at least mindim singular values; 3) Drop all singular values smaller than err (no normalization applied to the singular values)

parameterdescription.para: a UniTensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
the number (at most) of singular values to keep.
the cutoff error (the singular values smaller than err will be truncated.)
if true, the left- and right- unitary matrices (isometries) are returned.

whether the error shall be returned. If return_err is true, then the largest error will be pushed back to the vector (The smallest singular value in the return singular values matrix $ S $.) If return_err is > 1, then the full list of truncated singular values will be returned.

at least this many singular values are kept in total. For a per-block lower bound (which also acts as the per-block sampling floor of the randomized SVD), use the overload that takes a min_blockdim vector.

the randomized SVD computes [(1 + oversampling_factor) * keepdim*d/D + oversampling_summand] singular values in each block before further truncating, where d is the block dimension and D the full tensor dimension (each being the minimum of row and column dimension of the block or tensor respectively).
see oversampling_summand
number of iterations for the power method: Y = (A * Adag)^power_iteration * A * Tin
the seed for the random generator. [Default] Using device entropy.

listitem.para: The first tensor is a 1-d tensor containing the singular values
If is_UvT is true, then the tensors $ U $ and $ V^\dagger $ will be pushed back to the vector.
If return_err is true, then the error will be pushed back to the vector.

simplesect.para: The rowrank and permutation must be set accordingly for a matrix interpretation of the UniTensor.
Gesvd_truncate();
Uses a full Gesvd if keepdim is as large as the rank of the tensor

memberdef.argsstring: (const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, cytnx_uint64 keepdim, const std::vector< cytnx_uint64 > min_blockdim, double err=0., bool is_U=true, bool is_vT=true, unsigned int return_err=0, cytnx_uint64 mindim=1, cytnx_uint64 oversampling_summand=10, double oversampling_factor=1., cytnx_uint64 power_iteration=0, unsigned int seed=random::__static_random_device())
memberdef.name: Rsvd

briefdescription.para: Perform a truncated Singular-Value decomposition of a UniTensor and keep at most min_blockdim singular values in each block.

detaileddescription.para: For each block, the minimum dimension can be chosen for the truncation. This can be helpful to avoid loosing symmetry sectors in the truncated SVD. For more details, please refer to the documentation of the function Rsvd(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, cytnx_uint64 keepdim, double err = 0., bool is_U = true, bool is_vT = true, unsigned int return_err = 0, cytnx_uint64 mindim = 1, cytnx_uint64 oversampling_summand = 10, double oversampling_factor = 1., cytnx_uint64 power_iteration = 0, unsigned int seed = random::__static_random_device()).

The truncation order is as following (later constraints might be violated by previous ones): 1) Keep the largest min_blockdim singular values in each block; reduce keepdim and mindim by the number of already kept singular values 2) Keep at most keepdim singular values; there might be an exception in case of exact degeneracies where more singular values are kept 3) Keep at least mindim singular values; 4) Drop all singular values smaller than err (no normalization applied to the singular values)

parameterdescription.para: a vector containing the minimum dimension of each block; alternatively, a vector with only one element can be given to have the same min_blockdim for each block

simplesect.para: Rsvd(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, cytnx_uint64 keepdim, double err = 0., bool is_U = true, bool is_vT = true, unsigned int return_err = 0, cytnx_uint64 mindim = 1, cytnx_uint64 oversampling_summand = 10, double oversampling_factor = 1., cytnx_uint64 power_iteration = 0, unsigned int seed = random::__static_random_device())

briefdescription.para: Take the power p of all elements in a UniTensor.

parameterdescription.para: the input UniTensor
the power to take

simplesect.para: If Tin is a real UniTensor containing negative elements, then p must be an integer.
For symmetric UniTensors, only the elements in the blocks are inverted.
If p < 0 an inverse has to be taken. If elements could be zero (or close to it), use Inv(const UniTensor &Tin, double clip) for a pseudo-inverse instead.

For fermionic UniTensors, the sign structure of the blocks remains unchanged. This way, permute commutes with Pow. This can lead to unexpected behavior since T.Pow(2.0) can differ by signs from T * T.
Compared Pow_(UniTensor &Tin, const double &p), this function does not modify the input UniTensor but returns a new UniTensor.

briefdescription.para: Take the power p of all elements in a UniTensor, inplacely.

parameterdescription.para: the UniTensor
the power to take

simplesect.para: If Tin is a real UniTensor containing negative elements, then p must be an integer.
For symmetric UniTensors, only the elements in the blocks are inverted.
If p < 0 an inverse has to be taken. If elements could be zero (or close to it), use Inv_(UniTensor &Tin, double clip) for a pseudo-inverse instead.

For fermionic UniTensors, the sign structure of the blocks remains unchanged. This way, permute commutes with Pow_. This can lead to unexpected behavior since T.Pow_(2.0) can differ by signs from T * T.
Compared Pow(const UniTensor &Tin, const double &p), this is an inplace function, which modifies the input UniTensor.
Pow(const UniTensor &Tin, const double &p),

briefdescription.para: Element-wise (pseudo-)inverse.

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs an element-wise inverse with clip. If $ |T_\text{in}[i]| \le \mathrm{clip} $, then the new element is set to zero. \[ T_\text{out}[i] = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 1/(T_\text{in}[i]) & \mathrm{if} \ |T_\text{in}[i]| > \mathrm{clip} \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{array} \right. \]
Tin
the input UniTensor
clip
elmements with absolute value <= clip are set to zero, corresponding to the pseudo-inverse
UniTensor
For complex type UniTensors, the norm $ \sqrt{Re^2 + Im^2} $ is used to determine the clip.
If Tin is integer type, it will automatically be promoted to Type.Double.
For symmetric UniTensors, only the elements in the blocks are inverted.
Compared to Inv_(cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, double clip), this function does not modify the input UniTensor but returns a new UniTensor.
Inv_(UniTensor &Tin, double clip)
Inv(const Tensor &Tin, const double &clip)

briefdescription.para: Element-wise (pseudo-)inverse, inplacely.

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs an element-wise inverse with clip. If $ |T_\text{in}[i]| \le \mathrm{clip} $, then the new element is set to zero. \[ T_\text{in}[i] \rightarrow \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 1/(T_\text{in}[i]) & \mathrm{if} \ |T_\text{in}[i]| > \mathrm{clip} \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{array} \right. \]
Tin
the UniTensor
clip
elmements with absolute value <= clip are set to zero, corresponding to the pseudo-inverse
UniTensor
For complex type UniTensors, the norm $ \sqrt{Re^2 + Im^2} $ is used to determine the clip.
If Tin is integer type, it will automatically be promoted to Type.Double.
For symmetric UniTensors, only the elements in the blocks are inverted.
Compared to Inv(const UniTensor &Tin, double clip), this is an inplace function, which modifies the input UniTensor.
UniTensor Inv(const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, double clip)
Inv_(Tensor &Tin, const double &clip)

briefdescription.para: The addition function for Tensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the addition function between two Tensor. It will perform the element-wise addition. That means if the left Tensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the right Tensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_L[i] + T_R[i], \] where $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the Tensor $ T_L $ and $ T_R $. It will perform the element-wise addition and note that it will return a new Tensor object.
Lt
The left Tensor.
Rt
The right Tensor.
The result Tensor.
The shape of Lt and Rt must be the same.
Add(const T &lc, const Tensor &Rt), Add(const Tensor &Lt, const T &rc), iAdd(Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt), operator+(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The addition function for Tensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the addition function between a Tensor and a template type. It will perform the element-wise addition. That means if the template type lc is given as $ c $ and the Tensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = c + T_L[i], \] where $ T_R[i] $ is the elements in the Tensor $ T_R $. It will perform the element-wise addition and note that it will return a new Tensor object.
lc
The left template type.
Rt
The right Tensor.
The result Tensor.
Add(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt), Add(const Tensor &Lt, const T &rc), iAdd(Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt), operator+(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The addition function for Tensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the addition function between a Tensor and a template type. It will perform the element-wise addition. That means if the Tensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the template type rc is given as $ c $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = T_L[i] + c, \] where $ T_L[i] $ is the elements in the Tensor $ T_L $. It will perform the element-wise addition and note that it will return a new Tensor object.
Lt
The left Tensor.
rc
The right template type.
The result Tensor.
Add(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt), Add(const T &lc, const Tensor &Rt), iAdd(Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt), operator+(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The addition function for Tensor, inplacely.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the inplace addition function between two Tensor. It will perform the element-wise addition. That means if the left Tensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the right Tensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_L[i] = T_L[i] + T_R[i], \] where $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the Tensor $ T_L $ and $ T_R $. It will perform the element-wise addition and note that it will modify the left Tensor Lt.
Lt
The left Tensor.
Rt
The right Tensor.
The shape of Lt and Rt must be the same.
Compare to the function Add(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt), this is a inplace function and it will modify the left Tensor Lt.
Add(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt), Add(const T &lc, const Tensor &Rt), Add(const Tensor &Lt, const T &rc), operator+(const Tensor &Lt, const Tensor &Rt)

briefdescription.para: The comparison function for Tensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This is the comparison function between two Tensor. It will perform the element-wise comparison. That means if the left Tensor Lt is given as $ T_L $ and the right Tensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = \left\{\begin{array}{ll} 1, & \text{if } T_L[i]=T_R[i]\\ 0, & \text{else} \end{array}\right. \] where $ T_o[i]$, $ T_L[i] $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the Tensor $ T_o$, $ T_L $ and $ T_R $ and $ T_o[i] $ is the output Tensor which is a boolean type (see cytnx::Type). It will perform the element-wise comparison and note that it will return a new Tensor object.
Lt
The left Tensor.
Rt
The right Tensor.
The result Tensor.
The input tensors Lt and Rt should have the same shape.
Cpr(const T &lc, const Tensor &Rt), Cpr(const Tensor &Lt, const T &rc)

briefdescription.para: The comparison function for Tensor.

detaileddescription.para:

That means if the left template type lc is given as $ c $ and the right Tensor Rt is given as $ T_R $, then the result will be: \[ T_o[i] = \left\{\begin{array}{ll} 1, & \text{if } c=T_R[i]\\ 0, & \text{else} \end{array}\right. \] where $ T_o[i]$, $ c $ and $ T_R[i] $ are the elements in the Tensor $ T_o$, $ c $ and $ T_R $ and $ T_o[i] $ is the output Tensor which is a boolean type (see cytnx::Type). It will perform the element-wise comparison and note that it will return a new Tensor object.
lc
The left template type.
Rt
The right Tensor.
The result Tensor.

It will perform the element-wise comparison and note that it will return a new Tensor object.

briefdescription.para: Calculate the norm of a tensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This function will calculate the norm of a tensor. If the input tensor is rank-1, then the frobenius norm will be calculated. If the input tensor is rank-N with N>=2, then the tensor will be flatten (see flatten()) to 1d first, and then calculate the frobenius norm.
Tl
input Tensor
Tensor

briefdescription.para: Calculate the norm of an UniTensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This function will calculate the norm of an UniTensor. If the input UniTensor is rank-1, then the frobenius norm will be calculated. If the input UniTensor is rank-N with N>=2, then the blocks will be flatten (see flatten()) to 1d first, and then calculate the frobenius norm.
uTl
input UniTensor
Tensor

briefdescription.para: Calculate the determinant of a tensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This function will calculate the determinant of a square matrix . That means the input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor with shape (N,N).
Tl
input a Tensor with shape (N,N)
Tensor
the input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor with shape (N,N). (a square matrix)

memberdef.name: Rand_isometry

briefdescription.para: Generate an isometrized left isometry for a rank-2 Tensor (a matrix), such that Q * Qdag * Tin approximates Tin (Qdag is the hermitean conjugate of Q)

detaileddescription.para:
The power iteration algorithm with Gaussian random matrices is used: 1) Generate Gaussian random matrix X 2) Y = Tin * X 3) QR factorization: Y = Q * R 4) [U', S, vT] = SVD(Qdag * Tom) where Qdag is the hermitian conjugate of Q 5) U = Q * U' If power_iteration > 0, additional steps are inserted between 3) and 4): 3.1) Y = Tdag * Q where Tdag is the hermitian conjugate of Q 3.2) QR factorization: Y = Q * R 3.3) Y = Tin * Q 3.4) QR factorization: Y = Q * R steps 3.1) to 3.4) are repeated power_iteration times; this power method results in an exponential convergence of the singular values and is especially suitable if the singular values decay slowly. The result will depend on the rowrank of the UniTensor Tin. For more details, please refer to the documentation of the function Gesvd(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_UvT).
Tin
a Tensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
keepdim
the number of singular values to keep. Note that this is the size of the subspace, and the smallest singular values are less reliable. Use truncation with oversampling.
power_iteration
number of iterations for the power method: Y = (A * Adag)^power_iteration * A * Tin
seed
the seed for the random generator. [Default] Using device entropy.
Using power_iteration > 0 increases memory and time requirements, but leads to significantly improved precision!

briefdescription.para: Perform Singular-Value decomposition on a rank-2 Tensor (a matrix).

detaileddescription.para: This function will perform Singular-Value decomposition on a matrix (a rank-2 Tensor). That means given a matrix Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = U S V^\dagger, \] where $ U $ is a left uniform matrix, $ S $ is a diagonal matrix with singular values, and $ V^\dagger $ is the conjugate transpose of the right uniform matrix $ V $. Furthermore, $ U $ and $ V $ are unitary matrices, and $ S $ is a non-negative diagonal matrix.

parameterdescription.para: a Tensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
whether need to return a left unitary matrix.

listitem.para: The first tensor is a 1-d tensor contanin the singular values
If is_UvT is true, then the tensors $ U,V^\dagger $ will be pushed back to the vector.

simplesect.para: The input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix).

para.ref: Svd_truncate(const Tensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const double &err, const bool &is_UvT, const unsigned int &return_err, const cytnx_uint64& mindim)

That means given a matrix Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = U S V^\dagger, \] where $ U $ is a left uniform matrix, $ S $ is a diagonal matrix with singular values, and $ V^\dagger $ is the conjugate transpose of the right uniform matrix $ V $. Furthermore, $ U $ and $ V $ are unitary matrices, and $ S $ is a non-negative diagonal matrix.

parameterdescription.para: a Tensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
whether need to return left unitary matrix.
whether need to return right unitary matrix

listitem.para: The first tensor is a 1-d tensor contaning the singular values
If is_U is true, then the tensor $ U $ will be pushed back to the vector, and if is_vT is true, $ V^\dagger $ will be pushed back to the vector.

simplesect.para: The input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix).

para.ref: Gesvd_truncate(const Tensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const double &err, const bool &is_U, const bool &is_vT, const unsigned int &return_err, const cytnx_uint64& mindim)

memberdef.argsstring: (const Tensor &Tin, const cytnx_uint64 &keepdim, const double &err=0., const bool &is_UvT=true, const unsigned int &return_err=0, const cytnx_uint64 &mindim=1)
memberdef.name: Svd_truncate

briefdescription.para: Perform a truncated Singular-Value decomposition of a rank-2 Tensor (a matrix).

detaileddescription.para: This function will perform a truncated Singular-Value decomposition of a matrix (a rank-2 Tensor). It will perform the full SVD first, and then truncate the singular values to the given cutoff err. That means, given a matrix Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = U S V^\dagger, \] where $ S $ is a singular values matrix with the singular values truncated to the given cutoff err. The dimension of $ S $ is at most keepdim.

The truncation order is as following (later constraints might be violated by previous ones): 1) Keep at most keepdim singular values; there might be an exception in case of exact degeneracies, see note below 2) Keep at least mindim singular values; 3) Drop all singular values smaller than err (no normalization applied to the singular values)

parameterdescription.para: a Tensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
the number (at most) of singular values to keep.
the cutoff error (the singular values smaller than err will be truncated.)
if true, the left- and right- unitary matrices (isometries) are returned.

whether the error shall be returned. If return_err is true, then the largest error will be pushed back to the vector (The smallest singular value in the return singular values matrix $ S $.) If return_err > 1, then the full list of truncated singular values will be returned.

listitem.para: The first tensor is a 1-d tensor containing the singular values
If is_UvT is true, then the tensors $ U $ and $ V^\dagger $ will be pushed back to the vector.
If return_err is true, then the error will be pushed back to the vector.

simplesect.para: The input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix).

The truncated bond dimension can be larger than keepdim for degenerate singular values: if the largest $ n $ truncated singular values would be exactly equal to the smallest kept singular value, then the bond dimension is enlarged to keepdim $ + n $. Example: if the singular values are (1 2 2 2 2 3) and keepdim = 3, then the bond dimension will be 5 in order to keep all the degenerate singular values.

memberdef.argsstring: (const Tensor &Tin, cytnx_uint64 keepdim, double err=0., bool is_U=true, bool is_vT=true, unsigned int return_err=0, cytnx_uint64 mindim=1, cytnx_uint64 oversampling_summand=10, double oversampling_factor=1., cytnx_uint64 power_iteration=0, unsigned int seed=random::__static_random_device())
memberdef.name: Rsvd

briefdescription.para: Perform a truncated Singular-Value decomposition of a rank-2 Tensor (a matrix).

detaileddescription.para: This function will perform a truncated Singular-Value decomposition of a matrix (a rank-2 Tensor). It uses the ?gesvd method for the SVD. It will perform the full SVD first, and then truncate the singular values to the given cutoff err. That means, given a matrix Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = U S V^\dagger, \] where $ S $ is a singular values matrix with the singular values truncated to the given cutoff err. The dimension of $ S $ is at most keepdim.

The truncation order is as following (later constraints might be violated by previous ones): 1) Keep at most keepdim singular values; there might be an exception in case of exact degeneracies, see note below 2) Keep at least mindim singular values; 3) Drop all singular values smaller than err (no normalization applied to the singular values)

parameterdescription.para: a Tensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
the number (at most) of singular values to keep.
the cutoff error (the singular values smaller than err will be truncated.)
if true, the left- and right- unitary matrices (isometries) are returned.

whether the error shall be returned. If return_err is true, then the largest error will be pushed back to the vector (The smallest singular value in the return singular values matrix $ S $.) If return_err is a positive int, then the full list of truncated singular values will be returned.
at least this many singular values are kept in total.

the randomized SVD computes [(1 + oversampling_factor) * keepdim*d/D + oversampling_summand] singular values in each block before further truncating, where d is the block dimension and D the full tensor dimension (each being the minimum of row and column dimension of the block or tensor respectively).
see oversampling_summand
number of iterations for the power method: Y = (A * Adag)^power_iteration * A * Tin
the seed for the random generator. [Default] Using device entropy.

listitem.para: The first tensor is a 1-d tensor containing the singular values
If is_UvT is true, then the tensors $ U $ and $ V^\dagger $ will be pushed back to the vector.
If return_err is true, then the error will be pushed back to the vector.

simplesect.para: The input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix).
Gesvd_truncate();
Uses a full Gesvd if keepdim is as large as the rank of the tensor

memberdef.name: Hosvd

briefdescription.para: Perform QR decomposition on a rank-2 Tensor.

detaileddescription.para: This function will perform QR decomposition on a matrix (a rank-2 Tensor). That means given a matrix Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = Q R, \] where $ Q $ is a orthogonal matrix, and $ R $ is a right-upper triangular matrix.

parameterdescription.para: a Tensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (a matrix)
if return the tau that contains the Householder reflectors that generate q along with r. The tau array contains scaling factors for the reflectors

listitem.para: the first tensor is the orthomormal matrix $ Q $, a 2-d tensor (matrix)
the second tensor is the right-upper triangular matrix $ R $, a 2-d tensor (matrix)
the third tensor is the Householder reflectors $ H $, a 1-d tensor (vector). This tensor will only return when is_tau = true.

simplesect.para: The input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix).

briefdescription.para: Perform QDR decomposition on a rank-2 Tensor.

parameterdescription.para: a cytnx::Tensor, it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
if return the tau that contains the Householder reflectors that generate q along with r. The tau array contains scaling factors for the reflectors

listitem.para: the first tensor is the orthomormal matrix $ Q $, a 2-d tensor (matrix)
the second tensor is the diagonal matrix $ D $, a 1-d tensor (diagonal matrix)
the third tensor is the right-upper triangular matrix $ R $, a 2-d tensor (matrix)
the forth tensor is the Householder reflectors $ H $, a 1-d tensor (vector). This tensor will only return when is_tau = true.

simplesect.para: The input tensor should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix).

briefdescription.para: eigen-value decomposition for Hermitian matrix

detaileddescription.para: This function will perform eigen-value decomposition for Hermitian matrix. Given a matrix Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = V D V^\dagger, \] where $ V $ is a unitary matrix contains the eigen vectors, and $ D $ is a diagonal matrix contains the eigen values.

parameterdescription.para: a Tensor , it should be a rank-2 tensor (matrix)
whether need to return the eigen vectors.
if set to ture, the return eigen vectors will be row form.

simplesect.para: [std::vector<Tensors>], the first tensor is the eigen values, a 1-d tensor (vector). The second tensor is the eigen vectors, a 2-d tensor (matrix). This tensor will only return when is_V = true. Furthermore, if row_V = true, then the eigen vectors will be row form. Otherwise, the eigen vectors will be column form.
the Tin should be a Hermitian matrix.
If Tin is not a Hermitian matrix, only the lower triangular matrix will be used. (This is strongly not recommended, please use Eig(const Tensor &Tin, const bool &is_V, const bool &row_v) instead.

briefdescription.para: eigen-value decomposition for generic square matrix

detaileddescription.para: This function will perform eigen-value decomposition for generic square matrix. Given a matrix Tin as $ M $, then the result will be: \[ M = V D V^{-1}, \] where $ V $ is a invertible matrix contains the eigen vectors, and $ D $ is a diagonal matrix contains the eigen values.

parameterdescription.para: A square matrix (a rank-2 Tensor)
whether need to return the eigen vectors.
if set to ture, the return eigen vectors will be row form.

simplesect.para: [std::vector<Tensors>], the first tensor is the eigen values, a 1-d tensor (vector). The second tensor is the eigen vectors, a 2-d tensor (matrix). This tensor will only return when is_V = true. Furthermore, if row_V = true, then the eigen vectors will be row form. Otherwise, the eigen vectors will be column form.
the Tin should be a square matrix.

The returned eigenvectors $ V $ are in general only invertible, NOT unitary: the reconstruction is $ M = V D V^{-1} $, not $ V D V^\dagger $. The underlying LAPACK geev routine does not orthonormalize the eigenvectors, so $ V $ is generally non-unitary even when Tin is Hermitian/normal. Use Eigh for a unitary eigen basis.

briefdescription.para: perform trace over index.

This function will perform trace over index axisA and axisB. For example, if Tn is a rank-4 tensor $ T $, then axisA = 0 and axisB = 2, then the result will be: \[ \mathrm{Tr}_{j,l}(T) = \sum_{i,k} T_{i,j,k,l} \]
Tn

axisA

axisB

the Tn should be at-least rank-2 Tensor.

This function will perform matrix inverse on the input matrix Tin.
[Tensor] the inversion of the input matrix.

briefdescription.para: inplace matrix inverse.

detaileddescription.para:
This function will perform matrix inverse on the input matrix Tin, inplacely.
Compare to InvM, this is inlpace function. The input matrix will be modified to it's inverse.
the Tin should be a rank-2 Tensor.

briefdescription.para: Element-wise (pseudo-)inverse.

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs an element-wise inverse with clip. If $ |T_\text{in}[i]| \le \mathrm{clip} $, then the new element is set to zero. \[ T_\text{out}[i] = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 1/(T_\text{in}[i]) & \mathrm{if} \ |T_\text{in}[i]| > \mathrm{clip} \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{array} \right. \]
Tin
the input Tensor
clip
elmements with absolute value <= clip are set to zero, corresponding to the pseudo-inverse
Tensor
For complex type Tensors, the norm $ \sqrt{Re^2 + Im^2} $ is used to determine the clip.
If Tin is integer type, it will automatically be promoted to Type.Double.
Compared to Inv_(Tensor &Tin, const double &clip), this function does not modify the input UniTensor but returns a new UniTensor.
Inv_(Tensor &Tin, const double &clip)

briefdescription.para: Element-wise (pseudo-)inverse, inplacely.

detaileddescription.para:
This function performs an element-wise inverse with clip. If $ |T_\text{in}[i]| \le \mathrm{clip} $, then the new element is set to zero. \[ T_\text{in}[i] \rightarrow \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 1/(T_\text{in}[i]) & \mathrm{if} \ |T_\text{in}[i]| > \mathrm{clip} \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{array} \right. \]
Tin
the Tensor
clip
elmements with absolute value <= clip are set to zero, corresponding to the pseudo-inverse
Tensor
For complex type Tensors, the norm $ \sqrt{Re^2 + Im^2} $ is used to determine the clip.
If Tin is integer type, it will automatically be promoted to Type.Double.
Compared to Inv(const Tensor &Tin, const double &clip), this is an inplace function, which modifies the input Tensor.
Inv(const Tensor &Tin, const double &clip)

This function take the complex conjugate of all the elements in Tensor Tin. That is, the output will be: \[ T_{o}[i] = T_{in}[i]^* \] Furthermore,
if the input Tensor is complex, then return a new Tensor with all the elements are conjugated.
if the input Tensor is real, then return a copy of input Tensor.

memberdef.name: Kron

briefdescription.para: perform kronecker produces of two Tensor.

detaileddescription.para:
This function will perform kronecker produces of two Tensor Tl and Tr. Furthermore, the function assume two tensor has the same rank. In case where two tensors have different ranks, the small one will be extend by adding redundant dimension to the beginning of axis (T<x>_pad_right=true) or by adding redundant dim to the last axis (if T<x>_pad_left=false [default]). if the Tensor #1 has shape=(i1,j1,k1,l1...), and Tensor #2 has shape=(i2,j2,k2,l2...); then the return Tensor will have shape=(i1*i2,j1*j2,k1*k2...)
Tl

Tr
rank-m Tensor #2
Tl_pad_left
The padding scheme for Tl if Tl.rank != Tr.rank
Tr_pad_left
The padding scheme for Tr if Tl.rank != Tr.rank
[Tensor]
two tensor should on same device.

memberdef.argsstring: (LinOp *Hop, const Tensor &Tin=Tensor(), const std::string which="LM", const cytnx_uint64 &maxiter=10000, const cytnx_double &cvg_crit=0, const cytnx_uint64 &k=1, const bool &is_V=true, const cytnx_int32 &ncv=0, const bool &verbose=false)
memberdef.name: Arnoldi

briefdescription.para: Performs Arnoldi iteration for matrices or linear functions.

detaileddescription.para:
This function calculates the eigenvalue problem using the Arnoldi algorithm. It calls ARPACK routines.
Hop
The Linear Operator defined by the LinOp class or its inheritance (see LinOp).
Tin
The initial vector; this should be a rank-1 Tensor.
which
Specifies the order in which eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors should be found. The supported options are:
"LM": Largest Magnitude
"LR": Largest Real part
"SR": Smallest Real part
"LI": Largest Imaginary part
"SI": Smallest Imaginary part
maxiter
The maximum number of iteration steps.
cvg_crit
The convergence criterion of the energy. If set to 0, it reaches machine precision.
k
The number of eigenvalues to compute.
is_V
If set to true, the eigenvectors will be returned.
ncv
Related to number of Arnoldi Vectors generated (the Krylov subspace). If 0 (default), the value is typically determined automatically as min(2*k+10, dim), where dim is the dimension of the linear operator (e.g., nx in LinOp).
verbose
Prints out iteration information.
A std::vector<Tensor> containing: The first Tensor holds the computed eigenvalues. If is_V is true, subsequent Tensor objects contain the corresponding eigenvectors.
The data type of LinOp should be float and same as input inital vector.
1 <= k <= nx. Where nx is the dimension of the vector.
Tin should be a rank-1 Tensor, and the dimension need to same as LinOp->nx().
If ncv $ \neq 0$, then k+2<= ncv<= dim, where dim = LinOp->nx().
To use, define a linear operator with LinOp class either by assigning a custom function or creating a class that inherits LinOp (see LinOp for further details).
If k is close to the dimension of the vector, the routine may return error (arpack routine -3). In such cases, it is recommended to use the linalg::Eig function directly.
For the which equal to SI or LI, and the data type of LinOp is real rather than complex, the output of eigenvectors are same as ARPACK. For example, if the full spectrum is $[1.0+0i, 0.7+0.1i, 0.7-0.1i, 0.2+0.2i, 0.2-0.2i]$, and you choose which="LI", k=2, then the output eigenvalues are $[0.2+0.2i, 0.2-0.2i]$ rather than $[0.2+0.2i, 0.7+0.1i]$ (as ARPACK prioritizes returning complete complex conjugate pairs for real operators).

memberdef.argsstring: (LinOp *Hop, const cytnx::UniTensor &Tin, const std::string which="LM", const cytnx_uint64 &maxiter=10000, const cytnx_double &cvg_crit=1.0e-9, const cytnx_uint64 &k=1, const bool &is_V=true, const cytnx_int32 &ncv=0, const bool &verbose=false)
memberdef.name: Arnoldi

briefdescription.para: Performs Arnoldi iteration for matrices or linear functions.

detaileddescription.para:
This function calculates the eigenvalue problem using the Arnoldi algorithm. It calls ARPACK routines.
Hop
The Linear Operator defined by the LinOp class or its inheritance (see LinOp).
Tin
the initial UniTensor.
which
Specifies the order in which eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors should be found. The supported options are:
"LM": Largest Magnitude
"LR": Largest Real part
"SR": Smallest Real part
"LI": Largest Imaginary part
"SI": Smallest Imaginary part
maxiter
The maximum number of iteration steps.
cvg_crit
The convergence criterion of the energy. If set to 0, it reaches machine precision.
k
The number of eigenvalues to compute.
is_V
If set to true, the eigenvectors will be returned.
ncv
Related to number of Arnoldi Vectors generated (the Krylov subspace). If 0 (default), the value is typically determined automatically as min(2*k+10, dim), where dim is the dimension of the linear operator (e.g., nx in LinOp).
verbose
Prints out iteration information.
[eigvals (UniTensor), eigvec_1, eivec_2, ..., eigvec_k]. The first UniTensor contains eigenvalues.
The initial UniTensor cannot be empty.
The data type of LinOp should be float and same as input inital vector.
1 <= k <= nx. Where nx is the dimension of the vector.
Tin should be a rank-1 Tensor, and the dimension need to same as LinOp->nx().
If ncv $ \neq 0$, then k+2<= ncv<= dim, where dim = LinOp->nx().
To use, define a linear operator with LinOp class either by assigning a custom function or creating a class that inherits LinOp (see LinOp for further details).
If k is close to the dimension of the vector, the routine may return error (arpack routine -3). In such cases, it is recommended to use the linalg::Eig function directly.
For a fermionic (BlockFermionicUniTensor) input, the user-supplied LinOp::matvec MUST implement the operator action with the correct fermionic signs (e.g. apply fermion_twists() to the appropriate operand before the contraction). The iteration keeps every Krylov vector in the physical (signflip-resolved) frame and the inner products are then correct automatically, but the correctness of H|v> itself is entirely the LinOp's responsibility.
For the which equal to SI or LI, and the data type of LinOp is real rather than complex, the output of eigenvectors are same as ARPACK. For example, if the full spectrum is $[1.0+0i, 0.7+0.1i, 0.7-0.1i, 0.2+0.2i, 0.2-0.2i]$, and you choose which="LI", k=2, then the output eigenvalues are $[0.2+0.2i, 0.2-0.2i]$ rather than $[0.2+0.2i, 0.7+0.1i]$ (as ARPACK prioritizes returning complete complex conjugate pairs for real operators).

the Linear Operator defined by LinOp class or it's inheritance (see LinOp).

the initial vector, this should be rank-1.

Hop

the initial vector, this should be rank-1.
which
Specifies the order in which eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors should be found. The supported options are:

maxiter

cvg_crit

k

is_V

ncv
Related to number of Arnoldi Vectors generated (the Krylov subspace). If 0 (default), the value is typically determined automatically as min(2*k+10, dim), where dim is the dimension of the linear operator (e.g., nx in LinOp).
verbose

[eigvals (Tensor), eigvecs (Tensor)(option)]

memberdef.name: Lanczos_Exp

briefdescription.para: Perform the Lanczos algorithm for hermitian operator $H$ to approximate $e^{H\tau}v$.

detaileddescription.para:
This function perform the Lanczos-like algorithm for hermitian linear operator $H$ to approximate \[ e^{H\tau}v \] and return the state $w$ such that \[ |\exp(H\tau)v - w| < \delta. \] Here $v$ is a given vector or a state.
Hop
the Linear Operator defined by LinOp class or it's inheritance (see LinOp). The operation method $Hv$ need to be defined in it.
v
The input vector (or state). The norm $|v|$ should be equal to 1.
tau
A scalar, it can be complex number.
CvgCrit
$\delta$, the convergence criterion.
Maxiter
the maximum interation steps for each k.
verbose
print out iteration info.
UniTensor $w$
To use, define a linear operator with LinOp class either by assign a custom function or create a class that inherit LinOp (see LinOp for further details)
User need to guarantee that the input operator $H$ is Hermitian , and the exponetiate $e^{-H\tau}$ will converged. Ohterwise, the function will return the wrong results without any warning.

briefdescription.para: Return the least-squares solution to a linear matrix equation.

detaileddescription.para: Computes the vector x that approximatively solves the equation A @ x = b. The equation may be under-, well-, or over-determined independent columns. If a is square and of full rank, then x (but for round-off error) is the "exact" solution of the equation. Else, x minimizes the Euclidean 2-norm $ || b - a x ||_2 $.

parameterdescription.para: "Coefficient" matrix, must be two-dimensional.
Ordinate or "dependent variable" values, must be two-dimensional, the least-squares solution is calculated for each of the K columns of b.

Cut-off ratio for small singular values of a. For the purposes of rank determination, singular values are treated as zero if they are smaller than rcond times the largest singular value of A, If it is negative, the machine precision is used.

simplesect.para: [std::vector<Tensors>]

listitem.para: the first tensor is least-squares solutions in the K columns.
the second tensor is the sums of squared residuals: Squared Euclidean 2-norm for each column in b - a @ x. If the rank of a is < N or M <= N, this is a zero Tensor.
the third tensor is the rank of matrix A.
the forth tensor is singular values of A.

a

x

y

detaileddescription.para: see https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/introducing-batch-gemm-operations.html This function performs tensor type check and type conversion, then call the corresponding blas function.

briefdescription.para: linear algebra related functions.

detaileddescription.para:
This namespace contains all the linear algebra related functions. For example, the matrix multiplication, the singular value decomposition, etc. If the linear algebra can only perfom on a matrix, then in most cases,
If the object is Tensor, then it need to be rank-2.
If the object is UniTensor, then the result will depend on the UniTensor's rowrank.
